The sand bulges where oversized crabs have buried themselves beneath torn weed and shells. They burst up together when warm feet cross the tidemark.
Dark fins pace the surf beside a line of jagged rocks. The raiders wait for a wave to break hard, then rush through the foam with spears low.
A wounded gull drops into knee-deep water and vanishes in a red flash. The circling shark has learned that panicked splashing brings easier prey.
Shells and weed have arranged themselves into a pattern that repeats along the strand. Reading the tide sign points toward safe ground before the water turns.
A narrow shelf drops away beneath clear water, and the returning wave pulls hard against ankles and packs. Anyone who fights it wrong is dragged across sharp shells.
An abandoned crab pot knocks against a post with each wave. Inside, a waxed purse has tangled in the rope among claws and broken shells.
A column of damp smoke rises from a spit cut off by the tide. Whoever lit it has little time before the next wave front swallows the sandbar.
Tracks from a shuttered beacon lead across wet sand toward sea caves. Without the missing lens, ships on this coast will run blind tonight.
A clutch of large eggs lies half exposed where storm wind stripped the dune grass. Keeping them safe means deciding what deserves protection when the parent returns.
Low thorny vines carry tart berries above the dry sand. The ripe fruit hides under leaves that also conceal small biting insects.
Crunchy saltwort grows where fresh water seeps through the dunes. A careful cut leaves the roots buried and the patch alive.
Long ribbons of kelp have washed ashore after a night storm. The freshest strands must be sorted from spoiled weed before the sun turns them foul.
Clear pools between the rocks hold crabs fattened on trapped minnows. They are easy to see but quick to wedge themselves beneath stone.
Small fish flash just beyond the first break of waves. Casting at the right moment lands a meal before the school moves down the coast.
A flat of wet sand shows tiny breathing holes where clams hide below. Digging fast matters because the returning tide quickly fills every mark.
Green and blue glass glitters among black pebbles at the storm line. The useful pieces are smooth, but broken shards still wait under the sand.
A shallow basin behind the dunes has dried into thick white crust. Lifting clean slabs takes care before damp sand fouls the harvest.
A wave-cut bluff exposes a band of clean red clay. The bank groans with each breaker, so the best clay must be taken quickly and lightly.
Heavy driftwood has lodged high above the tide after last night's storm. It is dry enough to use if freed before the sea reclaims it.
A section of hull lies half buried below the dunes, its nails rusted but its boards still sound. Pulling them free without splitting them takes patient leverage.
A wind-bent palm has dropped a straight, dry trunk after lightning split its crown. It can be cut into usable lengths before salt air softens it.
